macro_segments.title: The Basic Question: When is Sweeping Out Required—Only for Returning Food or Also for Leaving It On?

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna's Requirements and the Dispute Over Its Scope
Chananya's Position: No Sweeping Needed to Leave Food If Partially Cooked
The Gemara's Initial Question: Does the Mishna Require Sweeping Only for Returning or Also for Leaving?
Rabbi Chelbo's Distinction Between Inside the Oven (Tocha) and On Top (Al Gaba)
Analyzing Whether Rabbi Chelbo's Position Applies to Returning or Leaving Food
The Double Oven (Kirot Metamimot): Proof That Sweeping is Required for Leaving Food On

macro_segments.title: The Baraita Dispute: Different Versions of Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel on What May Be Left On the Flame

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's Strict Version: Nothing on the Flame, Only Hot Water with Sweeping
Rabbi Yehuda's Version: Hot Water and Food Debate, with Taking Off and Returning
Gemara's Analysis: Does Our Mishna Align with Rabbi Yehuda or a Third Opinion?
Resolving the Contradiction: Our Mishna as a Compromise—Like Rabbi Yehuda but Differing on When Sweeping is Needed

macro_segments.title: Leaning Against the Oven (Somech): Whether Sweeping is Needed to Place Food Beside Rather Than On the Flame

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Can You Place Food Nearby the Oven Without Sweeping?
Double Oven Baraita: Clarifying That Somech Does Not Require Sweeping

macro_segments.title: Ashes and Re-ignition: Whether Putting Ashes on a Flame (That Later Reignites) Permits Food on the Flame

micro_segments.title: The Tosefta: Ashes or Complete Burnout Permits Leaving Food On, but Not Inside the Oven Without Sweeping
Rav Safra's Teaching: Even When Ashes Reignite, Certain Actions Remain Permitted

macro_segments.title: Leaning Against the Oven (Somech): Whether Sweeping is Needed to Place Food Beside Rather Than On the Flame (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Limitation: Somech Works Against an Oven Wall, Not Directly Against Exposed Flame

macro_segments.title: Ashes and Re-ignition: Whether Putting Ashes on a Flame (That Later Reignites) Permits Food on the Flame (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Isaac bar Nachmani: If Ashes Reignite, Only Fully Cooked Food May Remain
Rabbah bar Bar Chana: Same Rule for Reignited Ashes, Even with Hot-Burning Retam Embers

macro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet and Rav Yochanan: The Lenient Opinion That Sweeping is Only Needed to Return Food, Not to Leave It

micro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet Citing Rabbi Yochanan: You May Leave Food (Even Uncooked) on an Untreated Flame
Rava's Observation: This Is Already Taught in the Mishna of the First Perek
How to Read Our Mishna: Sweeping Required Only for Returning, Not for Leaving
Rashi and Tosafot: The Ashkenazi Reliance on Chananya Despite Gemara's Apparent Contradiction

macro_segments.title: The Strict Opinion and Debate Over Mitztemek v'Yafeh Lo: Can You Leave Fully Cooked Food on an Open Flame If It Continues to Improve?

micro_segments.title: Rav Shmuel bar Yehuda's Stringent Version: Only Fully Cooked Permitted on Untreated Flame
The Dispute: Rav and Shmuel Say Mitztemek v'Yafeh Lo is Forbidden; Rabbi Yochanan Permits It
Regional Practice: Babylonian Amoraim Follow Different Standards Based on Locale
Rav Ashi's Uncertainty: Is Fried Fish Permitted Because of Mitztemek v'Yafeh Lo or Mitztemek v'Ra Lo?
Rav Nachman's Final Rule: Mitztemek v'Yafeh Lo Forbidden; Only Mitztemek v'Ra Lo Permitted

macro_segments.title: Practical Applications: Which Foods Are Considered Mitztemek v'Ra Lo (Getting Worse) and When May They Be Left On the Flame

micro_segments.title: The General Principle: Foods with Flour Deteriorate (Mitztemek v'Ra Lo); Most Foods Permitted If Fully Cooked
Vegetable Stews: Without Meat, Deterioration Occurs; With Meat, No Deterioration Unless Guests Are Expected
Specific Foods: Fig Dishes, Oatmeal, and Date Dishes All Deteriorate and May Be Left On
